---
title: "How do annual working hours influence labour productivity across countries over time?"
author: "Kale-Ab Bobe"
date: "`r Sys.Date()`"
output: html_document
editor_options: 
  markdown: 
    wrap: 72
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)

# Libraries
library(knitr)
library(tidyverse)
library(readr)
library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(gganimate)

```

# Introduction

This report attempts to answer following questions:

**How do annual working hours influence labour productivity across
countries?**\

**Is higher productivity associated with stronger economic growth?**

This dataset examines the relationship between labour productivity,
annual working hours, and economic growth across multiple countries from
2015 to 2023. The data was sourced from Our World in Data , which
compiles global economic indicators from institutions such as the World
Bank and OECD.

The dataset includes four variables: Country, annual hours worked,
labour productivity (GDP per hour worked), and GDP growth (%). Country
is a categorical variable identifying each nation, while the remaining
variables are numerical and measure labour input, economic efficiency,
and economic performance respectively. Together, these variables enable
analysis of how work intensity relates to productivity and overall
economic growth.

```{r , include=FALSE}
#load dataset 
data <- read_csv("productivity vs hours worked vs GDP growth.csv") 
variable_table <- data.frame( Variable = c("Country", "Hours_Worked", "Productivity", "GDP_Growth"), Description = c( "Country name (categorical variable)", "Average annual hours worked per worker", "GDP per hour worked (labour productivity)", "Annual GDP growth rate (%)" ) )

kable(variable_table, caption = "Table 1: Description of Variables")

n_obs <- nrow(data)
n_vars <- ncol(data)
```

The dataset contains `r n_obs` observations and `r n_vars` variables.

```{r display_code_screenshot, echo=FALSE, out.width="70%"}
knitr::include_graphics("Code.png")
```

```{r display_str, echo=TRUE}
# Show structure and first 2 rows
str(data[1:2, ])
```

```{r , include=FALSE}
# Select relevant variables and compute summary stats 
summary_table <- data %>% 
  select(Entity,
         `Annual working hours per worker`,
         `Productivity: output per hour worked`) %>%  
  group_by(Entity) %>%   
  summarise(
    avg_hours = mean(`Annual working hours per worker`, na.rm = TRUE),
    avg_productivity = mean(`Productivity: output per hour worked`, na.rm = TRUE)
  ) %>% 
  head(10)

kable(summary_table, caption = "Table 2: Average Hours Worked and Productivity by Country")
```

Countries with lower average working hours often display higher
productivity levels, suggesting a more **efficient** allocation of
labour resources. Additionally, working hours show a weak and inconsistent relationship with GDP growth across countries.

```{r}
p <- ggplot(data, aes(
  x = `Annual working hours per worker`,
  y = `Productivity: output per hour worked`,
  colour = Entity,
  size = `GDP growth observations`
)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.8) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE, colour = "black", linetype = "dashed") +
  labs(
    title = 'Working Hours vs Productivity (Year: {frame_time})',
    subtitle = "Dashed line indicates overall trend",
    x = "Annual Working Hours per Worker",
    y = "Productivity (GDP per Hour Worked)",
    size = "GDP Growth (%)",
    caption = "Enhanced animated scatter plot with trend line (2015–2023)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    legend.position = "right",
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 10)
  ) +
  transition_time(Year) +
  ease_aes('linear')

animate(p, nframes = 80, fps = 8)

```

\- There is a negative relationship between working hours and
productivity, where countries with fewer working hours tend to have
higher productivity levels and higher GDP growth.\
\
- There is noticeable dispersion in the data, suggesting that other
economic factors also influence productivity beyond working hours alone.

### Conclusion

Analysis of the dataset indicates that countries with **fewer annual
working hours but higher productivity** tend to experience stronger GDP
growth, highlighting that efficient labour use drives economic
performance more than sheer work volume. The scatter plot shows a
general negative correlation between working hours and productivity,
while GDP growth is higher in countries that balance moderate hours with
high productivity. Variations across countries and years suggest that
other factors—such as technology, policy, and investment—also influence
economic outcomes. Overall, the findings support the conclusion that
optimizing labour efficiency, rather than increasing work hours, is more
strongly associated with positive GDP growth, aligning with the research
question investigating how working hours and productivity relate to
economic performance across countries from 2015–2023.
